extra.message: User-supplied input from request.POST.get('first_name') is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec() without any validation or sanitization. This allows arbitrary Python code execution on the server, enabling full system compromise.

The 'p' GET parameter from request.GET.get('p') is directly joined into a filesystem path via os.path.join(base_path, filename) and opened without any path sanitization. An attacker can read arbitrary files from the server using directory traversal sequences like '../../settings.py'.

User-supplied URL from request.GET.get('url') is passed directly to Django's redirect() without any validation. An attacker can craft a link that redirects users to a malicious external site, enabling phishing attacks.

The missing_access_control view grants access to an admin page by simply checking request.GET.get('action') == 'admin' without any authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can access privileged admin functionality.

The dor_user_profile view allows any user to view and modify any other user's profile by changing the userid URL parameter. There is no authentication check and no ownership verification. An attacker can access admin accounts (userid=2) and modify their email/name without authorization.

The unvalidated_forward view uses globals().get(forward) with user-supplied input from request.GET.get('fwd') to dispatch to any global function, including the admin view. This allows an unauthenticated attacker to bypass access controls and invoke sensitive functions.

The 'path' URL parameter captured via regex (?P<path>.+) is injected directly into the template context without any HTML escaping or sanitization in the view. This allows reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks via the URL path.

User input from request.GET.get('qs') is passed directly into the template context without sanitization. The raw, unsanitized value is rendered in the template, enabling reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

User input from request.GET.get('qs') is injected directly into the template context and rendered into an HTML form field value attribute without sanitization. This enables reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via query parameter.

User input from request.GET.get('qs') is passed into the template context and rendered unsanitized. Combined with the @csrf_exempt decorator, this allows both reflected XSS and exploitation of CSRF vulnerabilities via script injection.
The @csrf_exempt decorator disables CSRF protection on the csrf_image view, making it vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks.

Django's CsrfViewMiddleware is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES, globally disabling CSRF protection for the entire application. All POST endpoints are vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks.

DEBUG is set to True, which causes Django to display detailed error pages with stack traces, local variables, and settings when exceptions occur. This exposes sensitive application internals to attackers.

The show_toolbar function enables the Django Debug Toolbar for any request containing a 'debug' parameter in GET, POST, or cookies. This exposes SQL queries, request headers, settings, template context, and other sensitive debugging information to any user who adds ?debug=1 to a URL.

Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in the source code. This key is used for cryptographic signing of sessions, CSRF tokens, and password reset tokens. If this code is committed to a repository, the key is exposed to anyone with access.
